topic: 9.2-Sigma Dashboard — One Constant, Not Twenty Tests
our_argument_summary: Section 4.1 argues the dome's 'Live Power' dashboard is not 20 independent 9.2σ tests because 14 of the 20 domains share the fitted constants λ_g = 8,619 km and H₀ = 8,537 km. The section tabulates each of the 20 domains, shows the globe predicts the same observable in 12/14 (and better in some), groups the remaining 6 as (B) known constants adopted as predictions and (C) testable-but-problematic, and closes with the 'one ruler, thirty rooms' analogy plus a cross-reference to Part 2b code_analysis statistics via {{CA_REVIEWED}}/{{CA_DOME}} placeholders.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: The dome's Live Power page (live.html) is not in the cloned raw-text dump — only the evolution log and predictions.txt mention individual WIN-level sigma-like thresholds. The 9.2σ figure and the 20-domain convergence number cannot be verified from the archived raw-text corpus.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Unknown from raw-text alone. EXP-014 (monitor/analyst/expansions/EXP-014-live-power-archive.json) was supposed to add raw-text/08-live-power.txt as a verifiability anchor, but the file is still absent from the repository.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: N/A at section level; applies to individual WINs.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: The section's 'Verifiability note' asserts that the full Live Power page content is 'preserved in our repository at raw-text/08-live-power.txt.' That file does NOT exist in the current main branch (confirmed via `ls raw-text/`). This is a factual citation to our own repository that any reader — hostile or otherwise — can disprove in seconds. EXP-014 recommended creating the file and was apparently never integrated.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Section-level item; not a WIN with its own monitor.py entry.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: N/A — this is our prose, not a dome claim.

holes_found.description: UNRESOLVED FROM CYCLE 1: The verifiability footnote still cites 'raw-text/08-live-power.txt' as preserving the dome's Live Power page, but that file does not exist in the repository. `ls raw-text/` shows only 01-home through 07-ai-context. The Cycle 1 review flagged this exact problem and EXP-014 was created to fix it, but the archive was never committed. A dome defender who clicks the sentence's claim will catch us making a false claim about our own repo — and will use it to dismiss the entire section's 'Verifiability note' framing.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) commit the archived Live Power page content as raw-text/08-live-power.txt (preferred — restores verifiability), or (b) rewrite the footnote to reference the EXP-014 archive JSON and the dynamic live.html URL, removing the fabricated file reference. Do one or the other before next build.

holes_found.description: UNRESOLVED FROM CYCLE 1: The '12 of 14 cases' count in the 'Key column' paragraph is inconsistent with the Group A table itself. The table's 'Globe Predicts Same?' column shows YES for 13 of 14 rows (only #8 Telluric Cutoff shows 'N/A' — dome contradicts data). The prose says '12 of 14 both predict same; #2 Tesla assumes spherical; #8 contradicts data,' which would be 12 + 1 + 1 = 14 but treats #2 as not-YES even though the table marks it YES. Readers comparing the table to the paragraph will notice the mismatch.
holes_found.recommendation: Either update the paragraph to '13 of 14' and treat #2 Tesla separately as an even stronger category (globe-confirming rather than non-discriminating), or change the table's row #2 'Globe Predicts Same?' entry to 'YES (globe-confirming)' so prose and table agree.

holes_found.description: Cross-WIN refraction inconsistency with WIN-065. SEC-4.1 argues Polaris's +0.27° excess falls within a budget that includes 'atmospheric refraction (~0.5° near the horizon, ~0.1° at high elevation).' WIN-065's detail_evidence, by contrast, states '~0.02–0.03° at typical Polaris elevations' and explicitly does not rely on refraction as a large component of the budget. At mid-latitudes (35–60°N) Polaris sits 35–60° above the horizon, where standard refraction tables give ~0.01–0.03°, not ~0.1°. A dome defender who reads both will accuse us of inflating the refraction term to make the budget look wider in SEC-4.1, while quietly using the correct (smaller) value in WIN-065.
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten SEC-4.1's Polaris paragraph to match WIN-065: the dominant error term at mid-latitudes is Polaris's 0.74° polar offset (time-dependent), with refraction contributing only ~0.02–0.03° and instrument uncertainty of ±0.2–0.5° the secondary term. The argument is still strong — the +0.27° falls well inside the 0.74° offset alone — and dropping the inflated refraction number makes us more credible.

holes_found.description: The section never explains HOW 9.2σ is computed. We assert '14 of 20 share a constant, therefore the 9.2σ is meaningless,' but the dome's aggregation method could be Fisher's combined-p, Stouffer's Z, a chi-square of residuals, or a weighted Bayesian update. Each has different sensitivity to non-independence. Without quoting the dome's actual aggregation formula, a reader can reasonably object that we're attacking a statistical bogeyman. The Cycle 1 review flagged this as 'statistical independence claim oversimplified' — the fix is small.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence (or a footnote) noting that the dome's aggregation method on live.html is [X — quote from the page], then state the non-independence critique applies regardless of aggregation method because the shared λ_g means the off-diagonal terms of the correlation matrix are not near zero. One extra sentence inoculates us against the 'you don't know what 9.2σ means' rebuttal.

holes_found.description: The 'one ruler in 30 rooms' analogy shifts to '30 rooms' while the actual count is 14 rooms. The mismatch is small but noticeable: '14 of 20 domains... imagine measuring your height in 30 rooms' is an arithmetic jolt. A careful reader stops to work out whether 30 is a typo.
holes_found.recommendation: Change '30 rooms' → '14 rooms' (matching the text), or explicitly say 'imagine measuring your height with the same ruler in many different rooms' without committing to a number.

holes_found.description: The κ (EM-gravity coupling) row in Group A says 'Globe Predicts Same? YES (null: expects 0.0)' — this is clearer than Cycle 1 but is still slightly odd phrasing. The globe does not 'predict' zero; it is silent on aetheric coupling, which is a non-standard concept. 'Null hypothesis (zero coupling) is what standard physics assumes and Membach confirmed' would be more precise.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'YES (null: expects 0.0)' with 'YES (standard physics: zero)' or 'Data favors null (Membach = 0.0 μGal).'

stronger_arguments.description: The section should cite the dome's own correlation matrix if live.html publishes one. If the dome tabulates cross-domain correlations (e.g., 'Domain 1 & Domain 4 correlated at r = 0.92 because both depend on λ_g'), that is the dome admitting against interest that the 14 domains are not independent. Using the dome's own numbers makes the argument unassailable.

The 'directionality' paragraph compares λ_g to G, but G has a stronger example: it was measured by Cavendish (1798) in a lab and later predicted Neptune's orbit, Shapiro delay, and LIGO ringdown frequencies — none of which were in Cavendish's training data. We should name at least one specific out-of-sample prediction from G to make the contrast concrete instead of abstract.
sources: Shapiro (1964) Phys. Rev. Lett. 13, 789 — radar time delay from GR

stronger_arguments.description: The section could add a simple degrees-of-freedom calculation: a 20-domain test with 14 domains sharing 2 fitted parameters (λ_g, H₀) has effective degrees of freedom of at most 20 − 14 × (1 − 1/14) ≈ 7 independent domains, not 20. Converting 20 → 7 drops the claimed 9.2σ by roughly a factor of √(20/7) ≈ 1.7 when aggregating Gaussian p-values, so the 'real' significance is closer to ~5.4σ even granting all the dome's assumptions — and much less once the globe-matching issue is added. A concrete number makes the critique feel quantitative rather than rhetorical.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome is genuinely trying to do something scientifically respectable: publish a dashboard, tabulate domains, claim an aggregate significance, pre-register tests. This is more than most fringe cosmologies attempt. Sharing a fitted constant across domains is also not per se illegitimate — standard physics does it constantly (G, c, ℏ all appear in dozens of equations).
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The legitimacy of shared constants depends entirely on directionality: a constant measured in context X and then successfully predicting context Y (with no refitting) is evidence. A constant fitted to contexts X₁..X₁₄ and then 'tested' against those same contexts is tautology. The dome's dashboard is the latter, and nothing on live.html (so far as we can verify) demonstrates out-of-sample success for λ_g.

advocate_mode.best_defense: I accept your six methodological principles. Let me steel-man the dome. First, shared parameters across equations is how physics works: c appears in Maxwell, in E=mc², in cosmological redshift, in the Schumann formula itself. Nobody calls that 'one test repeated'; they call it a theory with an economical parameter set. Your critique would, if consistently applied, dismiss the Standard Model's use of a single G across every gravitational domain. Second, the dome does claim out-of-sample prediction: λ_g was fitted to geomagnetic data and then used to predict Schumann suppression during geomagnetic storms, Mascon gravity anomalies at GRACE resolution, and the D-layer altitude — none of which were in the original fit. Whether those are 'surprising' is subjective, but the formal structure is the same out-of-sample use you praise G for. Third, your '12 of 14 non-discriminating' is not a refutation of the dome; it is a refutation of the ability of low-resolution observations to distinguish models. The dome's honest response is 'we agree — that's why we registered the eclipse and E-PRED-B as the discriminating tests.' You are attacking the non-discriminating domains while the dome is pointing at the discriminating ones. Fourth, you criticize the absence of 'a surprising confirmed prediction in an unrelated Domain Y,' but that is exactly what the August 2026 eclipse is supposed to be — a pre-registered prediction in a domain (eclipse magnetic perturbations) not used to fit λ_g. Judge the dome after August 2026, not before.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Add a paragraph acknowledging (a) that shared parameters are legitimate when the parameter is measured in one regime and used in another, and (b) that the dome's best candidate for out-of-sample prediction is the 2026 eclipse. Then preempt the rebuttal by noting that (i) λ_g was fit to Schumann AND geomagnetic AND gravity data simultaneously (not to one regime), so 'using it' in any of those domains is still in-sample; and (ii) the eclipse prediction's FSF = tan(lat)/tan(ref_lat) derivation (shown in Section 4.2) is itself a tautology that doesn't depend on λ_g, so success there would not vindicate the dashboard's 9.2σ claim. This collapses the advocate's 'wait for the eclipse' escape route.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: SEC-4.1's Polaris paragraph describes atmospheric refraction as ~0.1° at high elevation, while WIN-065's detail_evidence correctly states ~0.02–0.03° at typical Polaris altitudes. The section's Group A row #6 (Schumann Suppression) says 'both models predict suppression' — compatible with WIN-029's argument. Row #4 (Equatorial Gravity) says the dome 'uses the observed value as input — circular' — compatible with WIN-053's circular-derivation critique. No contradiction with WIN-002 (Tesla) or WIN-036 (NMP drift). The only cross-WIN inconsistency is the refraction magnitude for Polaris.

claims_checked: FSF = tan(lat)/tan(40°) derivation from H(r) = r tan(lat) and FSF = H(r)/r
FSF range 0.642–2.075 maps to latitude range 28.3°–60.1°N (consistent with European INTERMAGNET stations)
Fitting a 2-parameter exponential to 3 data points has 1 degree of freedom (correct: 3−2=1)
Effective independent-domain count when 14 of 20 share two parameters: roughly 6–8, not 20
quantitative_verification.computations: Verified algebraically: H(r) = r tan(lat) ⇒ H(r)/r = tan(lat), and normalization at ref_lat=40° gives tan(lat)/tan(40°). Python check: tan(50°)/tan(40°) = 1.4203; inverse-tan of dome's published FSF extremes (0.642, 2.075) yields 28.3° and 60.1°, matching the European station latitude band. Degrees-of-freedom algebra: 20 − 14 × (1 − 1/14) = 20 − 13 = 7 effective independent domains if the 14 are fully collinear in λ_g, which makes aggregate Gaussian significance fall by √(20/7) ≈ 1.69 — reducing a claimed 9.2σ to roughly 5.4σ.

deeper_analysis: On re-read with fresh eyes, the section's argumentative skeleton is strong and survives the steel-man test of Lens A at a rating of 3/5 — the dome defender's best comeback is 'judge the dashboard after the eclipse,' which is preemptable with one paragraph. The section's structural problems are all fixable with local edits: a missing raw-text file, an inconsistent 12 vs 13 count, an inflated refraction estimate, and a missing explanation of how 9.2σ is computed. The Cycle 1 review caught most of these; Cycle 2 confirms the top-line flaw (missing raw-text/08-live-power.txt) was never actually fixed. The EXP-014 expansion file exists but was apparently never integrated, which suggests a decider pipeline gap worth flagging to the tinker: an expansion can be 'written' without ever landing in the repo. The section does NOT have a verdict problem (there is no verdict field for sections), and the text's overall thesis is correct: 20 domains with one fitted ruler is not 20 tests. It's 1 test with 20 read-outs.

citations_unchecked: The exact 9.2σ aggregation formula — live.html content was not in raw-text; would require cross-check against current live page.
